planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or water, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; polka dot begonia requires consistent moisture but is highly susceptible to root rot in compacted, poorly drained, or consistently waterlogged media — allow the top inch to dry slightly between waterings.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; polka dot begonia is a Brazilian cane-type (angel wing) begonia that has become one of the most vigorously popular houseplants of recent years, with extraordinary social media visibility driven by its remarkable leaf pattern — the large, asymmetric, wing-shaped, olive-green to dark-green leaves are densely covered on the upper surface with a precise, even, all-over pattern of small, perfectly round, silver-white spots, while the undersides are uniformly deep maroon-red, creating a bicolor leaf effect that is striking from both sides. The plant grows with an upright, bamboo-like, cane-forming habit and can reach 4–5 feet indoors or in sheltered outdoor positions. Clusters of small, delicate, pale-pink to white, pendant flowers appear at intervals throughout the year, adding a floral bonus to the outstanding foliage display. It roots easily from stem cuttings in water. 'Wightii' is the most commonly available cultivar, with the densest and most even silver-spot pattern. An excellent outdoor specimen in sheltered shade gardens in zone 10–12.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich, freely draining potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; palm-leaf begonia tolerates lower humidity than most large begonias and is more drought-tolerant between waterings than the soft-stemmed types.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; palm-leaf begonia is a Brazilian cane begonia of exceptional architectural distinction — it grows with a tall, upright, woody-stemmed, clump-forming habit reaching 4–8 feet, and each leaf is dramatically and uniquely palmate (palm-shaped), divided into 7–16 long, narrow, deep-green, finger-like leaflets radiating from the leaf stalk like the fingers of an open hand or the fronds of a small palm, creating an unexpectedly tropical, palm-like silhouette that bears almost no resemblance to the typical begonia leaf form. The deeply cut, palmate leaves are themselves ornamentally distinctive and create a refined, unusual tropical foliage effect quite unlike any other begonia species. Periodically the plant produces large, pendulous clusters of small, white, fragrant flowers. The woody, bamboo-like stems develop a corky texture with age. An outstanding architectural begonia for shaded tropical patios, conservatories, and zone 10–12 gardens where its distinctive form can be displayed as a specimen.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes or offsets in spring, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; Tahitian spinach is one of the most productive and shade-tolerant edible aroids and will produce continuous leaf harvests in the partial-shade conditions of a tropical garden where most edible greens would fail in summer heat.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; Tahitian spinach (arrowleaf elephant ear, belembe) is a South American tropical aroid grown throughout the Pacific Islands, Caribbean, and tropical Africa as an important leafy vegetable — the large, glossy, arrow-shaped, deep-green leaves are regularly harvested and prepared like spinach or taro leaves: briefly cooked (the raw leaves contain irritating calcium oxalate crystals that disappear with cooking) in soups, stews, coconut milk curries, and as a wilted side vegetable. A mature plant can produce a steady supply of large, nutritious leaves throughout the warm season. It is one of the most productive leafy green vegetables for hot, humid, partially shaded growing conditions, thriving in the summer heat and partial shade that would cause spinach, chard, and most temperate leafy greens to bolt and fail. The plants spread by underground rhizomes and produce offsets that extend the colony. In zone 9–10 it is often frozen back in winter but regrows from the rhizomes in spring. An underused and highly productive tropical leafy vegetable for zone 9–12 edible gardens.

planting.note: Plant cormels (side shoots from the main corm) or divide corms in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, fertile, deep soil in partial shade after frost; malanga requires a long, warm growing season (8–10 months) and will not mature tubers properly if planted too late or harvested too early.

directions: Plant cormels or divide corms in moist, humus-rich, fertile, deep soil in partial shade; malanga (yautia, cocoyam, tannia, new cocoyam) is a tropical American root crop closely related to taro (Colocasia esculenta) and widely cultivated throughout the Caribbean, Central and South America, and tropical Africa as a major starchy staple food. The underground corms and cormels are large, starchy, and nutritious — boiled, baked, fried, or mashed in a broad range of Caribbean and Latin American dishes including Cuban malanga fritters (frituras de malanga), pasteles, soups (sancoche), and as a potato substitute. Unlike true taro, malanga corms are generally considered easier to digest and are less irritating to people with taro sensitivities. The above-ground plant is ornamental in itself — the large, arrowhead-shaped, deep-green leaves on tall, mottled petioles create an impressive tropical foliage display while the roots develop. It grows well in partial shade and moist, fertile, organically rich soil. Harvest corms 8–10 months after planting when the lower leaves begin to turn yellow. An important crop in Caribbean communities and a worthwhile tropical edible for zone 9–12 food gardens.

planting.note: Propagate from tissue culture or sow seeds (slow) or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade after frost; red Abyssinian banana does not produce offsets like Musa species and must be propagated by seed, division of the main trunk base, or tissue culture — it is a non-clumping, clonal specimen plant.

directions: Propagate from tissue culture or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade; red Abyssinian banana is the most dramatically colored of all ornamental banana plants — a non-clumping, monocarpic (flowers once then dies) Ethiopian banana species in the cultivar 'Maurelii' that produces enormous, paddle-shaped leaves (up to 8 feet long) in a spectacular combination of deep maroon-red midrib, burgundy-red leaf margins and undersides, and dark-green to olive-green central leaf surface, creating a massive, bold, jewel-toned tropical foliage effect that is unlike any other large-leaved tropical plant. The purple-red coloration is most vivid on young leaves emerging from the center and fades somewhat as each leaf matures, but the overall plant maintains its rich, dark, dramatic coloring throughout. Unlike fruiting bananas (Musa), Ensete ventricosum is a non-clumping banana species that grows from a single, non-suckering pseudostem to 10–15 feet in zone 10–12, producing leaves throughout its vegetative phase before the terminal inflorescence and dying after setting seed. In zone 9, it is often grown as a summer annual, started fresh from tissue culture plants each spring for its spectacular single-season tropical display.
